_note: Hand-authored executive summaries for each council member. Displayed on scorecard PDF above Performance Pillars. Update when a member's pattern changes materially. Each entry: archetype (short label) + summary (2-3 sentences, plain language, no acronyms).

OKeefe.summary: Shows up, stays on-topic, and asks about costs before voting. In a council that almost never votes no, O'Keefe is the closest thing to a member who consistently puts the taxpayer's question first. No grand gestures; just the work.

Blackaby.summary: More than a reliable vote. At the May 2026 Flock special meeting he engineered the compromise that broke a politically deadlocked issue: reject the full contract expansion, require competitive bidding, preserve the existing investigative tool. He cited concrete case evidence from his own district, publicly confronted the vendor on accountability failures, and extracted an on-record acknowledgment of past mistakes. Not a fiscal champion in the broader sense, but when a hard problem arrives he does actual work.

Bartlett.summary: Asks pointed fiscal questions in chambers and has a documented pattern of attaching co-author credit to proposals after the primary work is done. On issues where his constituents speak loudly and clearly — notably Flock, where he stated every constituent told him to vote no and he did — he follows through. Missed major fiscal votes including both budget adoptions. The constituent-following is real; whether it scales to structural fiscal problems his constituents can't easily organize around remains the open question.

Kesarwani.summary: Focused, present, and effective in chambers — but willing to reach for new revenue when the political path points that way. Authored the sales tax survey referral that launched Berkeley's 2026 tax campaign infrastructure. Not a fiscal champion, not a fiscal vandal.

Taplin.summary: Has built the institutional scaffolding for a systematic bond program — authoring referrals for regular bond issuance and leading infrastructure bond campaigns — on the premise that Berkeley should be borrowing more rather than reprioritizing the general fund. A genuine counterpoint: Taplin has been an effective champion for Automated License Plate Readers, navigating real political resistance to deliver a concrete public safety result. The exception doesn't change the governing disposition: new debt is his preferred tool.

Humbert.summary: The council's most verbose member (115 words per turn on average), with the highest investment in the existing homeless services apparatus. Shows up and engages substantively — the question is what the engagement is in service of. A homeless services special interests alignment score of 62 puts him firmly in the spending-orthodoxy camp.

Ishii.summary: The Mayor who has structured constituent access to prevent organized accountability — groups are excluded from her office hours — and whose transcripts contain near-zero fiscal concern language. Agenda-setter for a council that has never adopted the foundational fiscal balance test the City Auditor recommends.

LunaParra.summary: Highest homeless services special interests alignment score on the council, highest abstention rate, and a Taxpayer Alignment score near the floor. On the May 2026 Flock vote she split — YES on use policies and competitive RFP, NO on the existing contract extension — but dismissed a competing poll with 'when a poll isn't released, it usually means it didn't go your way,' applying an evidence standard she would not apply symmetrically. The abstention pattern and the selective evidence standards are consistent.

Tregub.summary: Third-highest fiscal concern rhetoric score — talks about fiscal challenges constantly in chambers — but a Taxpayer Alignment composite of zero. At the May 2026 Flock hearing, acknowledged that 'providing officers with effective tools is critically important' and then voted against the 12-month ALPR extension the competitive RFP process he endorsed required. Champions bond and sales tax campaigns through official constituent communications, lobbied for a county spending measure from his district email list, and scheduled a community input meeting three weeks after voting to approve the project it was nominally about.
